coredata.dc:title: Inhibition and disaggregation effect of flavonoid-derived carbonized polymer dots on protein amyloid aggregation
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Dexin
Wang, Sujuan
Dong, Jiawei
Li, Jie
Wang, Xinnan
Liu, Feng
Ba, Xinwu
coredata.dc:description: In this research, four water-insoluble flavonoid compounds were utilized and reacted with arginine to prepare four carbonized polymer dots with good water-solubility in a hydrothermal reactor. Structural characterization demonstrated that the prepared carbonized polymer dots were classic core-shell structure. Effect of the prepared carbonized polymer dots on protein amyloid aggregation was further investigated using hen egg white lysozyme and human lysozyme as model protein in aqueous solution. All of the prepared carbonized polymer dots could retard the amyloid aggregation of hen egg white lysozyme and human lysozyme in a dose-depended manner. All measurements displayed that the inhibition ratio of luteolin-derived carbonized polymer dots (CPDs-1) was higher than that of the other three carbonized polymer dots under the same dosage. This result may be interpreted by the highest content of phenolic hydroxyl groups on the periphery. The inhibition ratio of CPDs-1 on hen egg white lysozyme and human lysozyme reached 88 % and 83 % at the concentration of 0.5 mg/mL, respectively. CPDs-1 also could disaggregate the formed mature amyloid fibrils into short aggregates.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Flavonoid compound
Carbonized polymer dots
Amyloid fibrillation
Inhibition
Disaggregation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of the CPDs on protein amyloid aggregation
Disaggregation of CPDs on amyloid fibrils
Inhibition effect of CPDs on protein amyloid fibrillation
Effect of CPDs on the hydrophobic properties of protein
Effect of CPDs on the protein secondary structure
Disaggregation effect of CPDs on amyloid fibrils

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Foundation of Hebei Education Department (No. BJK2023004) and Post-graduate's Innovation Fund Project of Hebei University (HBU2023SS012).
This work was supported by Foundation of Hebei Education Department (No. BJK2023004) and Post-graduate\u2019s Innovation Fund Project of Hebei University (HBU2023SS012).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The image of TEM of CPDs-1. (b) XRD spectrum of CPDs-1. (c) FTIR spectra of CPDs-1. (d) FTIR spectra of CPDs-1, CPDs-2, CPDs-3 and CPDs-4.
(a) XPS survey spectrum and (b) C1s, (c) O1s, and (d) N1s high resolution spectra of CPDs-1. Comparison of XPS spectra (e) and O element content (f) for four CPDs.
(a) UV-vis absorption spectrum of luteolin (0.03 mg/mL) and CPDs-1 (0.06 mg/mL). (b) Fluorescence emission spectroscopy of CPDs-1 (0.06 mg/mL) at different excitation wavelength.

ThT fluorescence kinetics curve of (a) HEWL and (b) HL fibrillation with CPDs (0.05 mg/mL). The inhibition ratio of CPDs on (c) HEWL and (d) HL fibrillation. TEM images of HEWL amyloid fibrils (e) without CPDs and with (f) CPDs-1 (g) CPDs-2 (h) CPDs-3 (i) CPDs-4; TEM images of HL amyloid fibrils (j) without CPDs and with (k) CPDs-1 (l) CPDs-2 (m) CPDs-3 (n) CPDs-4 (0.5 mg/mL) after incubation for 6 days.

(a) ANS fluorescence emission spectra of native HEWL or HEWL samples with different CPDs (0.5 mg/mL) after incubation. (b) ANS fluorescence emission spectra of native HL or HL samples with different CPDs (0.5 mg/mL) after incubation. (c) Hot spot areas of HL and HEWL obtained by the AGGRESCAN server.

(a) CD spectra of HEWL and HEWL samples with CPDs (0.5 mg/mL) after incubation. (b) CD spectra of HL or HL samples with CPDs (0.5 mg/mL) after incubation. (c) Intrinsic fluorescence emission spectra of native HEWL, HEWL samples with CPDs (0.5 mg/mL) after incubation. (d) Intrinsic fluorescence emission spectra of native HL, HL samples with CPDs (0.5 mg/mL) after incubation.

ThT fluorescence data of HEWL (a) and HL (b) fibrils incubated with CPDs-1 at 60 ℃ for 48 h. TEM images of mature HEWL (c) and HL fibrils (d). TEM images of HEWL (e) and HL (f) sample after incubating with 0.5 mg/mL CPDs-1 at 60 ℃ for 48 h.

head.ce:title: Inhibition and disaggregation effect of flavonoid-derived carbonized polymer dots on protein amyloid aggregation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Hebei University, Baoding 071002, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Hebei University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Hebei University, Baoding 071002, P R China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this research, four water-insoluble flavonoid compounds were utilized and reacted with arginine to prepare four carbonized polymer dots with good water-solubility in a hydrothermal reactor. Structural characterization demonstrated that the prepared carbonized polymer dots were classic core-shell structure. Effect of the prepared carbonized polymer dots on protein amyloid aggregation was further investigated using hen egg white lysozyme and human lysozyme as model protein in aqueous solution. All of the prepared carbonized polymer dots could retard the amyloid aggregation of hen egg white lysozyme and human lysozyme in a dose-depended manner. All measurements displayed that the inhibition ratio of luteolin-derived carbonized polymer dots (CPDs-1) was higher than that of the other three carbonized polymer dots under the same dosage. This result may be interpreted by the highest content of phenolic hydroxyl groups on the periphery. The inhibition ratio of CPDs-1 on hen egg white lysozyme and human lysozyme reached 88 % and 83 % at the concentration of 0.5 mg/mL, respectively. CPDs-1 also could disaggregate the formed mature amyloid fibrils into short aggregates.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Protein misfolding and aggregation in the human organ and tissue system could lead to a series of serious neurodegenerative diseases, such as Alzheimer's [1,2], Parkinson's disease [3] and type Ⅱ diabetes mellitus [4,5]. The formed protein aggregates were named amyloid fibrils. It was well known that the formed amyloid fibrils were rich in β-sheet structure [6]. Until now, there was no effective therapy to cure these diseases. Retarding the formation of amyloid fibrils or destroying the formed amyloid fibrils were considered as effective methods for treatment of these protein diseases [7–9]. A large number of studies have been performed to synthesize or screen the inhibitor to prevent the formation of amyloid fibrils [10,11]. Nowadays, many synthesized and natural extracted compounds have been studied to evaluate their influences on the procedure of amyloid fibrillation [12,13].

Quantum dots, as a kind of nanomaterials, have received a lot of attention in recent years. It could be divided into inorganic and organic quantum dots. Inorganic quantum dots were usually utilized in semiconducting and optical fields [14,15]. However, they were rarely used in biomedical field due to their cytotoxicity to the human body. Organic quantum dots were mainly composed of carbon elements, and they were also known as carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [16]. They were extensively utilized in biological, medical and biochemical fields because of their good biocompatibility and non-cytotoxicity to the human body [17,18]. They were also reported as inhibitor on amyloid fibrillation of protein. Despite of these reports, the development of new CQDs-based inhibitor on amyloidosis still remained elusive and required further efforts.

Carbon quantum dots could be synthesized through top-down or bottom-up methods. In bottom-up methods, the precursor molecules firstly reacted to form polymer molecule and the polymer molecule was further carbonized under high temperature to obtain the carbon quantum dots [14,19]. These kinds of carbon quantum dots could be named carbonized polymer dots (CPDs). The structure of precursor could be partly inherited to CPDs and the properties of the CPDs were closely related to the precursor molecules, which was important for the design of CPDs as inhibitors on protein amyloid aggregation.

Flavonoid compound, as a kind of natural substance, possessed many pharmacological activities, such as anti-oxidant, anti-inflammatory, anti-cancer properties [5,9,20]. It was also reported that many flavonoid compounds could intervene the protein amyloid aggregation [21,22]. However, organic solvent must be added in the fibrillation experiment due to their poor water-solubility, which greatly limited their application as drugs in clinical medicine [23,24]. The application of flavonoid compounds in medicine as drugs would be broadened if their water-solubility was increased.

The objective of this research was to obtain flavonoid-derived CPDs with good water-solubility through green synthesis method and the bioactivities of flavonoid compounds also could be retained in the CPDs. Four CPDs were prepared using four flavonoid compounds with different number of phenolic hydroxyl groups as precursors, respectively. Meanwhile, arginine was added to react with flavonoid compounds to improve the water-solubility of prepared CPDs. The prepared CPDs, as we expected, showed good water-solubility. The influences of four CPDs on protein amyloid aggregation were further investigated using hen egg white lysozyme (HEWL) and human lysozyme (HL) as model protein in aqueous solution. All of these four CPDs could intervene the formation of amyloid fibrils and the inhibition capability was greatly related to the number of phenolic hydroxyl groups. Luteolin-derived carbonized polymer dots (CPDs-1) showed the highest inhibition ratio compared with the other three CPDs under the same experimental conditions. The inhibition ratio of CPDs-1 on HEWL and HL reached 88 % and 83 % at the concentration of 0.5 mg/mL, respectively. CPDs-1 could retard amyloid aggregation of the protein and also could disaggregate the formed mature amyloid fibrils into short aggregates. This study could provide a new strategy for treatment of amyloid related disease and open new avenue for application of CPDs in medical fields.

HEWL, HL and 8-anilino1-naphthalenesulfonic acid (ANS) were purchased from Merck & Co. (St Louis, Mo.). Thioflavin T (ThT) was received from J & K Chemicals (Beijing, China). Luteolin, apigenin, genistein were obtained from RHAWN (Shanghai, China). Chrysin was got from yuan-ye Bio-Technology Co. (Shanghai, China). L-arginine was received from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

Four natural flavonoid compounds (luteolin, genistein, apigenin, chrysin) were utilized as carbon source to synthesize carbonized polymer dots. The detailed procedure was as follows. Luteolin (0.2 mmol) and arginine (0.6 mmol) were added to NaOH (25 mM) aqueous solution and the solution was sonicated for 5 min. The mixed solution was transferred to a hydrothermal reactor and reacted at 200 ℃ for 6 h. After the reactor was cooled down, the solution was centrifuged for 15 min. The supernatant was filtered with a filter membrane (0.22 μm). The filtrate was further dialyzed using pure water to remove unreacted small molecules. The black carbonized polymer dots (named CPDs-1) were obtained after freeze-drying. The other three carbonized polymer dots, prepared from genistein, apigenin and chrysin, were named as CPDs-2, CPDs-3, CPDs-4, respectively.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) measurement: The morphology and size of the carbonized polymer dots was characterized using TEM measurement. The CPDs were dispersed in ethanol and a copper grid was dipped into the solution several times. After drying under room temperature, the morphology and size were observed on a TEM (Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, FEI, USA) with working voltage 200 KV.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement: XRD measurement was conducted on an X-ray diffraction spectrometer (D8 ADVANCE, Bruker, Germany) with CuKα as the source. Working voltage was 40 KV and working current was 40 mA. The measurement range was 10–70°.
X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement: XPS was measured using a Scientific ESCALAB QXi.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) measurement: FTIR spectroscopy was obtained on FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS10, Thermo, USA) using KBr pellet. Wave number was from 4000 to 400 cm−1. Resolution ratio was 0.4 cm−1 and the scan number was 32.

UV–vis absorption measurment: The UV–visible Spectroscopy was recorded on UV spectrometer (Analytikjena SPECORD-210, Analytikjena, Germany). The concentration of the CPDs was 0.06 mg/mL. Slit width was 2 nm.

Fluorescence measurement: The fluorescence spectra were performed on fluorescence spectrometer (F-7000, HITACHI, Japan). The concentration of the CPDs was 0.06 mg/mL. Both excitation slit and emission slit were 20 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the CPDs on protein amyloid aggregation
ce:section.ce:para: HEWL and HL were used as model protein to study the effect of CPDs on amyloid aggregation. Firstly, HEWL or HL (4 mg/mL) was dissolved in hydrochloric acid solution (pH=1.4) containing NaCl (140 mM), respectively. The mature amyloid fibrils were obtained after incubating at 60 ℃ for 6 days. Different concentration of the prepared CPDs were added to the protein samples and they were incubated at the same condition to investigate the effect of prepared CPDs on the protein amyloid aggregation.

The kinetics process of amyloid fibrillation was probed by ThT fluorescence measurement. ThT solution (0.01 mM) was prepared by dissolving ThT in phosphate buffer (10 mM, pH = 7.4). Incubated samples (60 μL) were taken out every 24 h and mixed with 2-mL ThT solution. The fluorescence intensity of mixed solution at 486 nm was measured and recorded with 450 nm excitation wavelength. The ThT fluorescence intensity of control sample after incubation for 6 days was considered as 1. The normalized ThT fluorescence intensity of every sample could be obtained by dividing the measured fluorescence intensity by the fluorescence intensity of the control sample after incubation for 6 days. The normalized data were fitted according to the following equation:[25]:
(1)
F =
F
0
+
1
1 − exp [ r
max ( t 1 / 2 − t ) ]
where F0 was the longitudinal intercept of the fitted curve. F was the ThT fluorescence intensity at t time. t1/2 referred to the time when the fluorescence intensity was half of maximum. rmax is the the maximum growth rate of amyloid fibril formation.

Intrinsic fluorescence measurement: HL or HEWL samples (100 μL) after incubation for 6 days were diluted with 2- mL phosphate buffer solution (10 μM, pH=7.4). The fluorescence spectra of the mixed solution were measured on a fluorescence spectrometer (F-7000, HITACHI, Japan). All spectra ranging from 300 to 450 nm with excitation at 280 nm were recorded. Excitation and emission slits were both set at 10 nm.

ANS fluorescence measurement: HL or HEWL samples (40 μL) after incubation for 6 days were mixed with ANS solution (2 mL, 20 μM) in phosphate buffer (10 mM, pH=7.4). The fluorescence spectra of the mixed solution were tested on a fluorescence spectrometer (F-7000, HITACHI, Japan). All spectra were recorded from 400 to 600 nm with excitation at 380 nm. Excitation slit was set at 20 nm and emission slit was set at 10 nm.

The change of protein secondary structure in the process of amyloid fibrillation was monitored by CD measurements. HEWL and HL samples incubated for 6 days were diluted 100 times with pure water. CD spectra were tested on a circular dichroism spectrometer (MOS-450 / SFM300, Bio-Logic Company). The step interval was set at 0.5 nm and the scan rate was 50 nm/s.

HEWL and HL samples after incubation for 6 days were first diluted with pure water. A copper grid was dipped into the samples solution several times. Then the samples on the copper grid were stained with 1 % (w/v) phosphotungstic acid. After drying, the morphology was observed using TEM measurement (Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, FEI, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Disaggregation of CPDs on amyloid fibrils
ce:section.ce:para: The model protein samples were incubated at 60 ℃ for 6 days to obtain the mature amyloid fibrils. The CPDs-1 of 0.5 mg/mL were added to the fibril samples and the mixed solutions were further incubated at 60 ℃. ThT fluorescence spectra and TEM measurement were further performed to probe the disaggregation effect of the CPDs-1 on HEWL and HL amyloid fibrils.
All data were obtained based on at least five dependent experiments and presented as mean.

Four flavonoid compounds (luteolin, genistein, apigenin, chrysin) with similar molecule structure and different number of phenolic hydroxyl groups were applied to synthesize carbonized polymer dots, respectively ( Fig. 1).

In addition, arginine was also added to improve the water-solubility of obtained carbonized polymer dots. Flavonoid compounds and arginine (molar ratio =1:3) in NaOH aqueous solution were heated to 200 ℃ for 6 h in a hydrothermal reactor. The prepared CPDs were named as CPDs-1, CPDs-2, CPDs-3 and CPDs-4, respectively. The solubility of four flavonoid compounds and the prepared CPDs in water was measured, respectively. The results were given in Table S2. The water-solubility of four CPDs was greatly improved comparing with the corresponding flavonoid compound. Herein, only the detail structure characterization spectra of CPDs-1 were given and the spectra of the other three CPDs were shown as supporting information.

The morphology of the prepared CPDs was characterized by the measurement of TEM ( Fig. 2a). All the CPDs were uniformly distributed and tori-spherical. The average particle sizes for CPD-1, CPDs-2, CPDs-3, CPDs-4 were 2.19 nm, 2.00 nm, 2.25 nm and 2.60 nm, respectively. A wide diffraction peak at 23° was observed in the XRD patterns of CPDs-1 (Fig. 2b). FT-IR spectra were measured to analyze the end functional groups (Fig. 2c, d). For luteolin, the band at 3427 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration OH group [26]. For arginine, the band at 1588 cm−1 was corresponded to the deformation vibration of NH2 group [27]. In the FTIR spectrum of CPDs-1, the bands at 3427 cm−1 and 1588 cm−1 were both observed, which demonstrated the presence of a large amount of OH and NH2 groups on the periphery of CPDs-1. The bands at 1640 cm−1, 1408 cm−1, 1199 cm−1 were corresponded to the CO stretching vibration, C-N stretching vibration, C-C-N bending vibration, respectively [28]. The analysis showed that both luteolin and arginine participated in the formation of CPDs-1. In addition, the FTIR spectra of these four CPDs were similar and the bands at 3500 cm−1 in CPDs-1 was the strongest (Fig. 2d), which demonstrated that the content of OH groups in CPDs-1 was highest in these four CPDs.

XPS was further performed to characterize the elements and the end groups. As displayed in the full XPS spectra ( Fig. 3a), CPDs-1 contained C (285.06 eV, C1s), N (399.82 eV, N1s), O (531.12 eV, O1s) three elements. In C1s high resolution spectra (Fig. 3b), three peaks at 284.61 eV, 285.95 eV and 287.82 eV were observed and were attributed to CC/C-C, C-O/C-N, CO/-CONH, respectively [29]. There were two peaks at 399.03 eV and 400.68 eV in the N1s high resolution spectra (Fig. 3c), corresponding to CN, C-N. For O1s, two peaks at 531.01 eV and 532.5 eV were originated from C-O and CO, respectively [30]. The presence of large quantity of N and O elements on the periphery endowed the CPDs-1 good water-solubility [31]. Comparing the XPS spectra of four CPDs (Fig. 3e), the intensity of the O1s peak (532.12 eV) in CPDs-1 was the strongest, which demonstrated that the O element content in CPDs-1 was highest in these four CPDs. These results were consistent with the analysis of FTIR spectra. The element content for these four CPDs from high resolution spectra were shown in Table S1. It could be found that the content of C-O (8.29 %) in CPDs-1 was actually higher than the other three CPDs (Fig. 3f). Considering the molecular structure of reactant, most of C-O came from the linkage of OH groups to the carbon atom. This demonstrated that the OH end groups of the flavonoid compounds were not destroyed in the process of reaction and inherited to the CPDs. The prepared CPDs was classic core-shell structure with hydrophobic carbon core and hydrophilic OH end groups on the periphery.

UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra measurement were utilized to characterize the optical properties of the CPDs. The spectra of CPDs-1 were displayed in Fig. 4 and the spectra of CPDs-2, CPDs-3, CPDs-4 were given in Fig. S1, Fig. S2, Fig. S3, respectively. In the UV–vis absorption spectra of luteolin, there were two absorption peaks. One peak at 208 nm was attributed to the π-π* transition and this peak was red-shifted to 223 nm in the spectra of CPDs-1 due to the presence of the longer conjugate structure [30]. The other weak shoulder peak at 264 nm was attributed to the n-π* transition and it was also red-shifted to 277 nm in the spectra of CPDs-1. For the other three CPDs, the absorption peaks were also red-shifted compared with the corresponding flavonoid compound. The fluorescence emission spectra of these four CPDs were greatly dependent on the excitation wavelength. For CPDs-1, CPDs-2 and CPDs-3, the maximum fluorescence emission intensity was observed at the excitation wavelength of 430 nm. For CPDs-4, the maximum fluorescence emission intensity was found at the excitation wavelength of 410 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibition effect of CPDs on protein amyloid fibrillation
ce:section.ce:para: It was reported that flavonoid compounds could retard the formation of amyloid fibrillation [32–34]. Herein, the effect of four flavonoid compounds and arginine on HEWL and HL amyloid aggregation was also investigated and the data were shown in Fig. S4. The results displayed that four flavonoid compounds showed different inhibition ratio on HEWL and HL amyloid aggregation. The inhibition ratio of luteolin was the highest, which may be caused by the most content of phenolic hydroxyl groups. Arginine showed almost no inhibition effect on HEWL and HL amyloid fibrillation. However, these flavonoid compounds with aromatic ring could not dissolve in aqueous solution and organic solvent must be added in the experiment of amyloid fibrillation [21,35].

Herein, four flavonoid compounds were used as precursors and reacted with arginine to prepare CPDs. The prepared CPDs with large quantity of OH end groups on the periphery could well dissolve in aqueous solution and the presence of phenolic OH end groups may endow CPDs the ability to suppress the amyloid aggregation [36]. Therefore, the effect of four CPDs on protein amyloid aggregation of HEWL and HL was investigated in aqueous solution. Both the kinetic process of HEWL and HL fibrillation could be divided into three stages, including the nucleation period, the fibrillation growth period and the final plateau period. When the same concentration of CPDs were added to incubated HEWL or HL samples, the final ThT fluorescence intensity after incubating for six days was lower than that of the samples without CPDs ( Fig. 5a, b). Moreover, the order of ThT fluorescence intensity value was CPDs-4>CPDs-3>CPDs-2>CPDs-1. The lowest ThT fluorescence intensity demonstrated the best inhibition ability of CPDs-1 on HEWL and HL amyloid fibrillation, which could be interpreted by the most content of OH end groups on the periphery of CPDs-1. The inhibition ratio was greatly dependent on the concentration of CPDs. At 0.05 mg/mL, the inhibition ratio of CPDs-1 on HEWL and HL was 59 % and 73 %, respectively. When the concentration increased to 0.5 mg/mL, the inhibition ratio of CPDs-1 on HEWL and HL reached 88 % and 83 %, respectively (Fig. 5c, d). In order to verify the importance of the flavonoid compound, the CPDs using arginine as sole carbon resource were also prepared and named Arg-CPDs. The effect of Arg-CPDs on HEWL and HL amyloid aggregation was also investigated. From Fig. 5c and d, the inhibition capability of Arg-CPDs were obviously lower than the other four CPDs.
TEM images were measured to observe the morphology of the fibrils. For both HEWL and HL samples, the mature hair-like fibrils were formed after incubating at

60 ℃ for 6 days (Fig. 5e, j). When the same concentration of CPDs (0.5 mg/mL) were added to the protein samples, there were some short amyloid aggregates for CPDs-2, CPDs-3 and CPDs-4. For CPDs-1, there were no any amyloid aggregates were found (Fig. 5f, k). These results demonstrated that CPDs-1 displayed the best inhibition capability on HEWL and HL amyloid aggregation, which was in line with the result of ThT fluorescence measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CPDs on the hydrophobic properties of protein
ce:section.ce:para: ANS could easily combine with the hydrophobic fragments of protein and the fluorescence intensity increased accompanying with the blue-shift of maximum fluorescence emission wavelength. Therefore, it was usually used as a fluorescence probe to monitor the change of hydrophobicity in the procedure of amyloid aggregation [37,38]. In Fig. 6(a, b), both of the native HEWL or HL samples showed weak ANS fluorescence intensity. The ANS fluorescence intensity greatly increased after the formation of amyloid fibrils. This result indicated that the hydrophobic fragments were gradually exposed from the inner structure to the solvent in the procedure of amyloid aggregation. When the HEWL or HL samples were incubated with the CPDs, the ANS fluorescence intensity was all lower than that of the samples without the CPDs. This demonstrated that the addition of the CPDs could suppress the formation of amyloid fibrils and the inhibition capability of the CPDs-1 was the best.

In the procedure of amyloid aggregation, some regions were exposed to the solvent and these regions prone to aggregate were named hot spot regions or amyloidogenic regions [38–40]. The hot spot areas of HL and HEWL were calculated using the AGGRESCAN server online and were displayed in Fig. 6c. It could be found that there were similar hot spot regions for HEWL and HL, which caused the analogous inhibition effect of CPDs on their amyloid aggregation. The hydrophobic carbon core of the CPDs may bind with the amyloidogenic regions of the protein and retard the procedure of amyloid aggregation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CPDs on the protein secondary structure
ce:section.ce:para: The protein secondary structure was changed during the amyloid aggregation and the change could be monitored by the measurement of CD spectra [35,41,42]. For native HEWL, there were two negative peaks at 208 nm and 222 nm, corresponding to the α-helical conformation ( Fig. 7a). After the amyloid fibrils were formed, these two negative peaks disappeared and another peak at 202 nm contributing to the β-sheet structure was observed, which demonstrated that the amyloid fibrils were rich of β-sheet structure. When the HEWL samples were incubated in the presence of CPDs, the CD peak shape was similar to that of the mature amyloid fibrils (Fig. 7c) and the intensity was lower than that of mature amyloid fibrils. This indicated that four CPDs could suppress the amyloid aggregation of HEWL and CPDs-1 showed the best inhibition capability. There were also two negative peaks at 208 nm and 222 nm for native HL and only one peak at 218 nm for mature HL fibril, demonstrating the conversion from α-helical to β-sheet conformation (Fig. 7b). After the CPDs were added to the incubated samples, the results for HL were the same as those for HEWL (Fig. 7d). These results were consistent with those of ThT and TEM measurement.

Tryptophan was usually utilized as a marker to investigate the structure change of protein. There were tryptophan groups in both HEWL and HL structure. Therefore, the measurement of intrinsic fluorescence spectra for HEWL and HL could be utilized to monitor the change of micro-environment during the amyloid aggregation [43]. For both HEWL and HL (Fig. 7c, d), the intrinsic fluorescence intensity was obviously decreased and the emission wavelength was red-shifted from 340 nm to 347 nm after the formation of amyloid fibrils, which indicated that the microenvironment around tryptophan was changed and the tryptophan groups were gradually exposed to the solvent during amyloid aggregation. After CPDs were added to the incubated samples, the CPDs may interact with the aromatic amino acid residues and the fluorescence intensity was greatly quenched. Meanwhile, the tryptophan groups were buried inside the structure, which induced the decrease of fluorescence intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Disaggregation effect of CPDs on amyloid fibrils
ce:section.ce:para: Many diseases are induced by the formation of amyloid fibrils in the body and the disaggregation of the formed mature amyloid fibrils may be an effective method for treatment of the related disease [44,45]. Herein, the disaggregation influence of CPDs-1 on mature amyloid fibrils was probed using ThT fluorescence and TEM measurement. The mature amyloid fibrils were further incubated with different concentration of CPDs-1 at 60 ℃ for 48 h. For both HEWL and HL fibrils, the ThT fluorescence intensity was dramatically decreased in the first hour and did not greatly change after one hour ( Fig. 8a, b). The disaggregating effect was independent of the concentration of CPDs-1, especially for HL. In TEM images, the mature amyloid HEWL and HL fibrils were both long hair-like filaments (Fig. 8c, d). The long filaments were disaggregated into short aggregates after incubating with 0.5 mg/mL of CPDs-1 (Fig. 8 e-f).

This indicated that the mature amyloid fibrils could be rapidly disaggregated by CPDs-1. CPDs-1 could interact with amyloid fibrils through hydrogen bond, electrostatic or hydrophobic interaction, which further caused the disrupt of the formed amyloid fibrils.

Flavonoid compounds could inhibit the protein amyloid aggregation, but their application in medicine was limited due to their poor water-solubility. In this study, four CPDs with good water-solubility were prepared using four flavonoid compounds with different number of phenolic hydroxyl groups and arginine as carbon source in a hydrothermal reactor, respectively. The influences of the obtained CPDs on HL and HEWL amyloid aggregation were detail investigated in aqueous solution. The results demonstrated that the anti-fibrillation property of the flavonoid compound was inherited to the CPDs. The luteolin-derived CPDs displayed the best inhibition capability and also could disaggregate the formed fibrils into short aggregates, which demonstrated that the phenolic hydroxyl groups played a crucial role in their inhibition effect on amyloid aggregation. This research could open new avenue for the synthesis of water-solubility CPDs and broaden their application as potential drugs for treatment of amyloid-related disease.

Xinnan Wang: Methodology, Investigation. Feng Liu: Visualization, Funding acquisition. Jiawei Dong: Software, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Jie Li: Methodology, Conceptualization. Dexin Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Sujuan Wang: Writing – original draft, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Xinwu Ba: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: All authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Foundation of Hebei Education Department (No. BJK2023004) and Post-graduate’s Innovation Fund Project of Hebei University (HBU2023SS012).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfb.2024.113928.
